title: Laying luxury vinyl tile click flooring
subtitle: Install LVT click planks or tiles on any flat, dry sub-floor
excerpt: How to lay luxury vinyl tile click flooring on a prepared sub-floor, covering acclimatisation, setting out the first row, maintaining the stagger pattern across the field, and finishing at thresholds and walls.
sourceNotes: British Standards BS 8204-6 (screeds and bases for LVT). Flooring Industry Training Association (FITA) guidance. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

glossaryTerms.definition: Luxury vinyl tile or luxury vinyl plank: a multi-layer flooring product consisting of a printed photographic layer under a clear wear layer, bonded to a rigid or semi-rigid vinyl core. Click LVT planks interlock without adhesive, making the floor floating and removable. The wear layer thickness (typically 0.3 to 0.55 mm) determines durability.

A floor that is not bonded or fastened to the sub-floor. The planks or tiles interlock with each other and the whole floor moves as a unit with temperature and humidity changes. An expansion gap around the perimeter allows for this movement.

A clear space of 8 to 12 mm around the perimeter of a floating floor and around any fixed obstacles (pipes, doorframes, columns) to allow the floor to expand and contract without buckling. Covered by skirting boards, beading, or threshold strips.

content.text: Click
LVT
is a
floating floor

that clicks together without adhesive and can be laid over most existing floor surfaces that are flat and dry. The system is more forgiving than laminate: LVT has a degree of flex and handles minor sub-floor imperfections better than a rigid laminate plank. It can be relaid or removed without damaging the sub-floor, which makes it suitable for rented properties.
What you will need: materials

Click LVT planks or tiles in the quantity required for the room area plus 10 per cent for cuts and waste. An underlay specified by the manufacturer: some LVT has underlay pre-attached; others require a separate thin acoustic underlay (1.5 to 2 mm). Spacers for the expansion gap (8 to 10 mm). A threshold strip where the floor meets an adjacent room at a different level or material. Self-adhesive foam strips to support the floor under any long unsupported spans.
Step 1, acclimatise and check the sub-floor

Allow the LVT packs to acclimatise in the room where they will be laid for at least 24 hours at normal room temperature. The vinyl core adjusts to the room temperature; laying cold planks in a warm room leads to expansion gaps appearing after installation. Check the sub-floor for flatness with the spirit level: more than 3 mm deviation over 1800 mm must be corrected with a floor-levelling compound before laying. Sweep the sub-floor clean and check for protruding nail heads or screw heads: sand them flush with the sanding block or drive them below the surface.
Step 2, set out the first row

The first row must be straight and parallel to the longest wall or the most visible line in the room (often the line of the main doorway). Dry-lay the first row without clicking it together and check it against the wall with the tape measure. If the wall is not straight, scribe and cut the plank edge to follow the wall rather than cutting a wedge-shaped gap along the row. Leave an
expansion gap
of 8 to 10 mm at all walls and fixed obstacles. Insert spacers at the wall to hold the gap while laying.
Step 3, lay the field planks

Click each plank into its neighbour at an angle of about 20 to 30 degrees, then press the plank flat to lock the joint. Use the rubber mallet to tap the plank into position if the joint does not click fully home. Stagger the end joints by at least 300 mm between rows (some manufacturers specify a minimum of one-third of a plank length). The stagger is both structural and visual: aligned end joints in adjacent rows look wrong and are weaker. Cut the last plank in each row to fit, using the off-cut to start the next row wherever it gives a suitable stagger.
Step 4, finish at doorways and thresholds

Undercut the door stops and architrave so the LVT slides beneath them: use a utility knife or a small saw held flat against a scrap of the LVT to mark the height, then cut along the mark. Fit a threshold strip where the LVT meets a different floor surface in the adjacent room. Choose a T-bar threshold where the two floors are at the same level, a ramp threshold where one is higher, or a square-edge strip where the LVT meets carpet. Fix the threshold strip to the sub-floor and click the LVT up to it.
Step 5, remove spacers and fit skirting or beading

Remove all spacers. Refit skirting boards if they were removed, or fix a 12 to 18 mm cover bead along the base of the existing skirting to cover the expansion gap. Fix the bead to the skirting, never to the floor; the floor must be free to move underneath the bead. Wipe the floor surface with a damp cloth to remove pencil marks and debris.
Variations

Rigid-core LVT (SPC: stone plastic composite) handles more sub-floor variation than standard LVT and is suitable for bathrooms and kitchens. It is slightly thicker and heavier, which makes it more stable but more difficult to cut with a knife alone (a mitre saw or jigsaw is faster). Some products have a waterproof click-lock joint: check the manufacturer's specification before using them in a wet room.
Care

Clean with a damp mop and a pH-neutral cleaner. Avoid steam mops: the heat and moisture penetrate the click joints and can swell the core. Protect the surface from furniture feet with felt pads. Direct sunlight can cause UV fading in standard LVT; check the manufacturer's UV-resistance rating for south-facing rooms.

attrs.symptom: Joints open up across the field after a few weeks
attrs.cause: Insufficient expansion gap left at the perimeter; floor cannot expand and pushes joints apart.
attrs.fix: Cut back the flooring at the wall edge to restore the 8 to 10 mm gap. Refit the skirting bead to cover the gap. If the floor has already buckled at a joint, that joint must be re-clicked after the perimeter gap is restored.

attrs.symptom: Planks will not click together and leave a visible gap at the joint
attrs.cause: A piece of grit, adhesive, or old flooring residue is sitting in the groove of the preceding plank.
attrs.fix: Lift the plank and clean the groove of the preceding row with the filling knife. Even a small fragment of debris prevents the tongue from seating fully in the groove.

attrs.symptom: Floor makes a hollow clicking or drumming noise underfoot
attrs.cause: High point in the sub-floor creating an unsupported section of floor; underlay missing or too thin.
attrs.fix: Identify the high point by sliding a long spirit level across the sub-floor before laying. Sand or skim the high spot to bring it within the 3 mm tolerance. If the floor is already laid, the noise generally reduces with use as the planks settle.
